Linear Attention and Deep SSMs

Prerequisites

This chapter builds on Transformers and Linear Dynamical Systems. The state space model perspective connects linear attention to the structured SSMs developed there.

Softmax attention is the engine of the Transformer, but its O(N2)O(N^2)O(N2) cost in sequence length limits its applicability to long sequences. A family of models — linear attention, structured state space models (SSMs), and their selective and learnable-rule variants — achieve O(N)O(N)O(N) computation by maintaining a fixed-size hidden state that is updated recurrently. This chapter develops the mathematics connecting these architectures, from the kernel view of Katharopoulos et al., 2020 through S4 Gu et al., 2022, Mamba Gu & Dao, 2023, DeltaNet Yang et al., 2024, and test-time training Yu et al., 2024.

Source
import torch
import torch.nn.functional as F
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from torch import nn

Standard Softmax Attention¶

Given queries Q∈RN×dQ \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times d}Q∈RN×d, keys K∈RN×dK \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times d}K∈RN×d, and values V∈RN×dV \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times d}V∈RN×d, the output of causal (autoregressive) softmax attention at position iii is:

yi=∑j=1iexp⁡ ⁣(qi⊤kj/d)vj∑j=1iexp⁡ ⁣(qi⊤kj/d).y_i = \frac{\sum_{j=1}^{i} \exp\!\left(q_i^\top k_j / \sqrt{d}\right) v_j}{\sum_{j=1}^{i} \exp\!\left(q_i^\top k_j / \sqrt{d}\right)}.yi​=∑j=1i​exp(qi⊤​kj​/d​)∑j=1i​exp(qi⊤​kj​/d​)vj​​.
(1)

In matrix form (with the causal mask Mij=I[j≤i]M_{ij} = \mathbb{I}[j \leq i]Mij​=I[j≤i]):

Y=softmax ⁣(QK⊤d+log⁡M)V,Y = \mathrm{softmax}\!\left(\frac{QK^\top}{\sqrt{d}} + \log M\right) V,Y=softmax(d​QK⊤​+logM)V,
(2)

where log⁡M\log MlogM sets future entries to −∞-\infty−∞. Computing this requires materializing the N×NN \times NN×N attention matrix, costing O(N2d)O(N^2 d)O(N2d) time and O(N2)O(N^2)O(N2) memory — prohibitive for long sequences.

Linear Attention: The Kernel Trick¶

Katharopoulos et al., 2020 observe that the softmax in attention is a kernel function:

exp⁡(q⊤k/d)=κ(q,k).\exp(q^\top k / \sqrt{d}) = \kappa(q, k).exp(q⊤k/d​)=κ(q,k).
(3)

For a general positive-definite kernel κ(q,k)=ϕ(q)⊤ϕ(k)\kappa(q, k) = \phi(q)^\top \phi(k)κ(q,k)=ϕ(q)⊤ϕ(k) with explicit feature maps ϕ:Rd→Rr\phi: \mathbb{R}^d \to \mathbb{R}^rϕ:Rd→Rr, the causal attention output becomes:

yi=∑j≤iϕ(qi)⊤ϕ(kj) vj∑j≤iϕ(qi)⊤ϕ(kj)=ϕ(qi)⊤∑j≤iϕ(kj)vj⊤ϕ(qi)⊤∑j≤iϕ(kj).y_i = \frac{\sum_{j \leq i} \phi(q_i)^\top \phi(k_j)\, v_j}{\sum_{j \leq i} \phi(q_i)^\top \phi(k_j)} = \frac{\phi(q_i)^\top \sum_{j \leq i} \phi(k_j) v_j^\top}{\phi(q_i)^\top \sum_{j \leq i} \phi(k_j)}.yi​=∑j≤i​ϕ(qi​)⊤ϕ(kj​)∑j≤i​ϕ(qi​)⊤ϕ(kj​)vj​​=ϕ(qi​)⊤∑j≤i​ϕ(kj​)ϕ(qi​)⊤∑j≤i​ϕ(kj​)vj⊤​​.
(4)

The crucial observation: ϕ(qi)\phi(q_i)ϕ(qi​) acts on the accumulated outer products ∑j≤iϕ(kj)vj⊤∈Rr×d\sum_{j \leq i} \phi(k_j) v_j^\top \in \mathbb{R}^{r \times d}∑j≤i​ϕ(kj​)vj⊤​∈Rr×d, which can be built up incrementally. Defining the hidden state and normalizer:

St=St−1+ϕ(kt)vt⊤∈Rr×d,zt=zt−1+ϕ(kt)∈Rr,S_t = S_{t-1} + \phi(k_t) v_t^\top \in \mathbb{R}^{r \times d}, \qquad z_t = z_{t-1} + \phi(k_t) \in \mathbb{R}^r,St​=St−1​+ϕ(kt​)vt⊤​∈Rr×d,zt​=zt−1​+ϕ(kt​)∈Rr,
(5)

the output at each step is:

yt=St⊤ϕ(qt)zt⊤ϕ(qt).y_t = \frac{S_t^\top \phi(q_t)}{z_t^\top \phi(q_t)}.yt​=zt⊤​ϕ(qt​)St⊤​ϕ(qt​)​.
(6)

This is a linear RNN with hidden state StS_tSt​ — no quadratic cost, no materializing the attention matrix. The computation is O(Nrd)O(N r d)O(Nrd) in time and O(rd)O(r d)O(rd) in memory (for the hidden state).

A simple feature map that keeps values positive is ϕ(x)=elu(x)+1\phi(x) = \mathrm{elu}(x) + 1ϕ(x)=elu(x)+1 applied elementwise, so r=dr = dr=d.

def feature_map(x):
    '''ELU+1 feature map; keeps activations non-negative.'''
    return F.elu(x) + 1


def linear_attention_parallel(Q, K, V):
    '''Causal linear attention in parallel (matrix) form. O(N^2 r d).

    Args:
        Q, K, V: (N, d) tensors
    Returns:
        Y: (N, d) output
    '''
    Qf = feature_map(Q)   # (N, r), r == d here
    Kf = feature_map(K)

    # Causal kernel matrix: A[i,j] = phi(q_i)^T phi(k_j), j <= i
    A = torch.tril(Qf @ Kf.T)                    # (N, N)
    Z = A.sum(dim=-1, keepdim=True).clamp(min=1e-6)
    return (A / Z) @ V


def linear_attention_recurrent(Q, K, V):
    '''Causal linear attention in recurrent form. O(N r d).

    Args:
        Q, K, V: (N, d) tensors
    Returns:
        Y: (N, d) output
    '''
    N, d = Q.shape
    Qf = feature_map(Q)
    Kf = feature_map(K)

    S = torch.zeros(d, d)   # hidden state: r x d  (r == d)
    z = torch.zeros(d)      # normalizer
    Y = torch.zeros(N, d)

    for t in range(N):
        S = S + torch.outer(Kf[t], V[t])    # rank-1 update
        z = z + Kf[t]
        denom = (Qf[t] @ z).clamp(min=1e-6)
        Y[t] = (Qf[t] @ S) / denom

    return Y


# Verify the two forms agree
torch.manual_seed(0)
N, d = 16, 8
Q = torch.randn(N, d)
K = torch.randn(N, d)
V = torch.randn(N, d)

Y_par = linear_attention_parallel(Q, K, V)
Y_rec = linear_attention_recurrent(Q, K, V)
print(f'Max difference between parallel and recurrent forms: {(Y_par - Y_rec).abs().max():.2e}')

Max difference between parallel and recurrent forms: 1.79e-07

Structured State Space Models¶

Continuous-Time SSMs¶

A linear time-invariant (LTI) state space model describes a continuous-time dynamical system:

h′(t)=A h(t)+B x(t),y(t)=C⊤h(t),h'(t) = A\, h(t) + B\, x(t), \qquad y(t) = C^\top h(t),h′(t)=Ah(t)+Bx(t),y(t)=C⊤h(t),
(7)

where h(t)∈Rph(t) \in \mathbb{R}^ph(t)∈Rp is the hidden state, x(t)∈Rx(t) \in \mathbb{R}x(t)∈R is the scalar input, and A∈Rp×pA \in \mathbb{R}^{p \times p}A∈Rp×p, B∈RpB \in \mathbb{R}^pB∈Rp, C∈RpC \in \mathbb{R}^pC∈Rp are parameters.

Discretizing with step size Δ\DeltaΔ (zero-order hold) gives the recurrent form:

ht=Aˉ ht−1+Bˉ xt,yt=C⊤ht,h_t = \bar{A}\, h_{t-1} + \bar{B}\, x_t, \qquad y_t = C^\top h_t,ht​=Aˉht−1​+Bˉxt​,yt​=C⊤ht​,
(8)

where Aˉ=eΔA\bar{A} = e^{\Delta A}Aˉ=eΔA and Bˉ=(Aˉ−I)A−1B\bar{B} = (\bar{A} - I) A^{-1} BBˉ=(Aˉ−I)A−1B.

Because Aˉ\bar{A}Aˉ is fixed, the output is a convolution of the input with the SSM kernel:

yt=∑s=0tC⊤Aˉt−sBˉ⏟Kˉt−sxs=(Kˉ∗x)t,y_t = \sum_{s=0}^{t} \underbrace{C^\top \bar{A}^{t-s} \bar{B}}_{\bar{K}_{t-s}} x_s = (\bar{K} * x)_t,yt​=s=0∑t​Kˉt−s​C⊤Aˉt−sBˉ​​xs​=(Kˉ∗x)t​,
(9)

where Kˉ=(C⊤Bˉ,  C⊤AˉBˉ,  C⊤Aˉ2Bˉ,…)\bar{K} = (C^\top \bar{B},\; C^\top \bar{A} \bar{B},\; C^\top \bar{A}^2 \bar{B}, \ldots)Kˉ=(C⊤Bˉ,C⊤AˉBˉ,C⊤Aˉ2Bˉ,…) is the SSM kernel (impulse response). This duality — recurrent for inference, convolutional for training — is the key computational advantage of SSMs.

S4 and the HiPPO Initialization¶

The S4S4S4 model Gu et al., 2022 builds on the HiPPO framework Gu et al., 2020, which initializes AAA as a structured matrix designed to optimally memorize the history of the input via polynomial projections. The HiPPO-LegS matrix has entries:

Ank={−(2n+1)1/2(2k+1)1/2n>k−(n+1)n=k0n<kA_{nk} = \begin{cases} -(2n+1)^{1/2}(2k+1)^{1/2} & n > k \\ -(n+1) & n = k \\ 0 & n < k \end{cases}Ank​=⎩⎨⎧​−(2n+1)1/2(2k+1)1/2−(n+1)0​n>kn=kn<k​
(10)

Computing eΔAe^{\Delta A}eΔA naively is O(p3)O(p^3)O(p3). S4 exploits the fact that HiPPO-LegS is a normal plus low-rank (NPLR) matrix to compute the SSM kernel in O(p+Nlog⁡N)O(p + N \log N)O(p+NlogN) time via the fast Fourier transform.

S5: Diagonal SSMs with Parallel Scans¶

S5S5S5 Smith et al., 2023 simplifies S4 by diagonalizing the state matrix: Λ=P−1AP\Lambda = P^{-1} A PΛ=P−1AP where Λ\LambdaΛ is diagonal. With h~t=P−1ht\tilde{h}_t = P^{-1} h_th~t​=P−1ht​, the recurrence decouples into ppp independent scalar recurrences:

h~t,i=λih~t−1,i+B~ixt,yt=Re(C⊤h~t).\tilde{h}_{t,i} = \lambda_i \tilde{h}_{t-1,i} + \tilde{B}_i x_t, \qquad y_t = \mathrm{Re}(C^\top \tilde{h}_t).h~t,i​=λi​h~t−1,i​+B~i​xt​,yt​=Re(C⊤h~t​).
(11)

Each mode decays independently at rate ∣λi∣|\lambda_i|∣λi​∣; stability requires ∣λi∣<1|\lambda_i| < 1∣λi​∣<1. S5 computes all NNN hidden states in parallel using an associative parallel scan, reducing training time from O(Np)O(Np)O(Np) sequential steps to O(plog⁡N)O(p \log N)O(plogN) parallel steps.

Source
def ssm_recurrent(x, A_bar, B_bar, C):
    '''Scalar-input LTI SSM in recurrent form.

    Args:
        x:     (N,) input sequence
        A_bar: (p, p) discrete state matrix
        B_bar: (p,) discrete input vector
        C:     (p,) output vector
    Returns:
        y: (N,) output sequence
    '''
    N = len(x)
    p = len(C)
    h = torch.zeros(p)
    y = torch.zeros(N)
    for t in range(N):
        h = A_bar @ h + B_bar * x[t]
        y[t] = C @ h
    return y


def ssm_convolutional(x, A_bar, B_bar, C):
    '''Scalar-input LTI SSM in convolutional form (for comparison).

    Args:
        x:     (N,) input sequence
        A_bar: (p, p) discrete state matrix
        B_bar: (p,) discrete input vector
        C:     (p,) output vector
    Returns:
        y: (N,) output sequence via direct convolution with kernel K
    '''
    N = len(x)
    # Build SSM kernel K[t] = C^T A_bar^t B_bar
    K = torch.zeros(N)
    Apow = torch.eye(len(C))
    for t in range(N):
        K[t] = C @ (Apow @ B_bar)
        Apow = A_bar @ Apow
    # Causal convolution via FFT
    K_pad = torch.cat([K, torch.zeros(N)])
    x_pad = torch.cat([x, torch.zeros(N)])
    y_full = torch.fft.irfft(torch.fft.rfft(K_pad) * torch.fft.rfft(x_pad))
    return y_full[:N]


torch.manual_seed(1)
N, p = 64, 4

# Simple stable diagonal A_bar (eigenvalues in (0, 1))
lam = torch.tensor([0.9, 0.8, 0.7, 0.6])
A_bar = torch.diag(lam)
B_bar = torch.randn(p)
C = torch.randn(p)
x = torch.randn(N)

y_rec  = ssm_recurrent(x, A_bar, B_bar, C)
y_conv = ssm_convolutional(x, A_bar, B_bar, C)
print(f'Max diff recurrent vs convolutional: {(y_rec - y_conv).abs().max():.2e}')

# Visualise the SSM kernel and a response
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(10, 3.5))
K_vals = [float(C @ (torch.linalg.matrix_power(A_bar, t) @ B_bar)) for t in range(N)]
axes[0].stem(K_vals[:32], markerfmt='C0o', linefmt='C0-', basefmt='k-')
axes[0].set_title('SSM impulse response $\\bar{K}_t$')
axes[0].set_xlabel('Lag $t$')

axes[1].plot(x.numpy(), label='input $x_t$', alpha=0.6)
axes[1].plot(y_rec.numpy(), label='output $y_t$', lw=2)
axes[1].set_title('SSM input/output')
axes[1].set_xlabel('Time step')
axes[1].legend()

plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig('ssm_demo.png', dpi=100, bbox_inches='tight')
plt.show()

Max diff recurrent vs convolutional: 2.38e-07

Selective State Space Models: Mamba¶

A fundamental limitation of LTI SSMs is that the transition matrices Aˉ\bar{A}Aˉ and Bˉ\bar{B}Bˉ are input-independent: every token is processed identically regardless of content. This prevents the model from selectively retaining or discarding information based on context.

Mamba Gu & Dao, 2023 introduces a selection mechanism by making BtB_tBt​, CtC_tCt​, and the discretization step Δt\Delta_tΔt​ functions of the input xtx_txt​:

ht=Aˉt ht−1+Bˉt xt,yt=Ct⊤ht,h_t = \bar{A}_t\, h_{t-1} + \bar{B}_t\, x_t, \qquad y_t = C_t^\top h_t,ht​=Aˉt​ht−1​+Bˉt​xt​,yt​=Ct⊤​ht​,
(12)

where Aˉt=eΔtA\bar{A}_t = e^{\Delta_t A}Aˉt​=eΔt​A, Bˉt=(Aˉt−I)A−1Bt\bar{B}_t = (\bar{A}_t - I) A^{-1} B_tBˉt​=(Aˉt​−I)A−1Bt​, and Δt,Bt,Ct\Delta_t, B_t, C_tΔt​,Bt​,Ct​ are computed from xtx_txt​ via small linear projections.

Because parameters now vary with ttt, the convolutional view no longer applies — the system is time-varying, and yty_tyt​ depends on all of x1,…,xtx_1, \ldots, x_tx1​,…,xt​ in a non-linear way. Training requires a selective scan: a hardware-aware parallel algorithm that exploits the structure of the recurrence without materializing intermediate states in memory.

The selection mechanism gives Mamba attention-like content-dependent routing while preserving the O(Np)O(N p)O(Np) cost of a recurrent model.

DeltaNet: The Delta Rule as Attention¶

Online Least Squares and the Delta Rule¶

Consider maintaining a weight matrix St∈Rd×dS_t \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times d}St​∈Rd×d that approximates a key–value associative memory: given key ktk_tkt​ we want Stkt≈vtS_t k_t \approx v_tSt​kt​≈vt​. A natural objective is the online least-squares loss:

ℓt(S)=12∥Skt−vt∥2.\ell_t(S) = \tfrac{1}{2}\|S k_t - v_t\|^2.ℓt​(S)=21​∥Skt​−vt​∥2.
(13)

Taking one step of gradient descent with step size βt∈(0,1]\beta_t \in (0, 1]βt​∈(0,1] gives the delta rule update:

St=St−1−βt∇Sℓt(St−1)=St−1−βt(St−1kt−vt)kt⊤.S_t = S_{t-1} - \beta_t \nabla_S \ell_t(S_{t-1}) = S_{t-1} - \beta_t (S_{t-1} k_t - v_t) k_t^\top.St​=St−1​−βt​∇S​ℓt​(St−1​)=St−1​−βt​(St−1​kt​−vt​)kt⊤​.
(14)

Rearranging:

St=(I−βtktkt⊤) St−1+βtvtkt⊤.\boxed{S_t = (I - \beta_t k_t k_t^\top)\, S_{t-1} + \beta_t v_t k_t^\top.}St​=(I−βt​kt​kt⊤​)St−1​+βt​vt​kt⊤​.​
(15)

This is a rank-1 correction: it subtracts the current memory’s prediction St−1ktS_{t-1} k_tSt−1​kt​ (scaled by βt\beta_tβt​) and adds the target vtv_tvt​, weighted by the same key ktk_tkt​.

Compare to linear attention:

Stlin=St−1+ϕ(kt)vt⊤.(no forgetting)S_t^{\text{lin}} = S_{t-1} + \phi(k_t) v_t^\top. \qquad \text{(no forgetting)}Stlin​=St−1​+ϕ(kt​)vt⊤​.(no forgetting)
(16)

DeltaNet replaces the identity transition with (I−βtktkt⊤)(I - \beta_t k_t k_t^\top)(I−βt​kt​kt⊤​): old memories associated with ktk_tkt​ are selectively overwritten, not merely accumulated.

DeltaNet as an Attention Model¶

DeltaNet Yang et al., 2024 uses the delta-rule hidden state and produces output by querying with qtq_tqt​:

yt=St⊤qt.y_t = S_t^\top q_t.yt​=St⊤​qt​.
(17)

With keys and queries ℓ2\ell_2ℓ2​-normalized (∥kt∥=∥qt∥=1\|k_t\| = \|q_t\| = 1∥kt​∥=∥qt​∥=1) and βt∈(0,1]\beta_t \in (0, 1]βt​∈(0,1] a learned gate (e.g., via sigmoid), DeltaNet is a gated linear RNN with a content-dependent forgetting mechanism. It is more expressive than linear attention (which has A=IA = IA=I) but cheaper than softmax attention (O(Nd2)O(N d^2)O(Nd2) vs. O(N2d)O(N^2 d)O(N2d)).

Training DeltaNet efficiently requires computing the delta-rule updates in parallel over the sequence. Yang et al., 2024 derive a chunk-wise algorithm based on the WY representation of Householder products that achieves this without sacrificing hardware efficiency.

Source
def deltanet_recurrent(Q, K, V, beta):
    '''DeltaNet recurrence: delta-rule hidden state queried by Q.

    Args:
        Q, K: (N, d) unit-normalized query / key tensors
        V:    (N, d) value tensors
        beta: (N,) gate values in (0, 1]
    Returns:
        Y:    (N, d) output
    '''
    N, d = Q.shape
    S = torch.zeros(d, d)
    Y = torch.zeros(N, d)
    for t in range(N):
        k, v, b = K[t], V[t], beta[t]
        # Delta rule: remove old association, write new one
        S = S - b * torch.outer(S @ k, k) + b * torch.outer(v, k)
        Y[t] = S.T @ Q[t]
    return Y


def linear_attention_recurrent_nodenom(Q, K, V):
    '''Linear attention without denominator normalisation (for direct comparison).'''
    N, d = Q.shape
    Qf = feature_map(Q)
    Kf = feature_map(K)
    S = torch.zeros(d, d)
    Y = torch.zeros(N, d)
    for t in range(N):
        S = S + torch.outer(Kf[t], V[t])
        Y[t] = Qf[t] @ S
    return Y


torch.manual_seed(7)
N, d = 32, 16
Q = F.normalize(torch.randn(N, d), dim=-1)
K = F.normalize(torch.randn(N, d), dim=-1)
V = torch.randn(N, d) * 0.5
beta = torch.sigmoid(torch.randn(N))   # learned gate

Y_delta = deltanet_recurrent(Q, K, V, beta)

# Hidden-state norms: DeltaNet should stay bounded, linear attention grows
Y_lin_nd = linear_attention_recurrent_nodenom(Q, K, V)

# Track norm of S over time for both
S_delta = torch.zeros(d, d)
S_lin   = torch.zeros(d, d)
norms_delta, norms_lin = [], []
Qf = feature_map(Q); Kf = feature_map(K)
for t in range(N):
    k, v, b = K[t], V[t], beta[t]
    S_delta = S_delta - b * torch.outer(S_delta @ k, k) + b * torch.outer(v, k)
    S_lin   = S_lin + torch.outer(Kf[t], V[t])
    norms_delta.append(S_delta.norm().item())
    norms_lin.append(S_lin.norm().item())

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 3.5))
ax.plot(norms_delta, label='DeltaNet $\\|S_t\\|_F$', color='tab:blue')
ax.plot(norms_lin,   label='Linear attn $\\|S_t\\|_F$', color='tab:orange', ls='--')
ax.set_xlabel('Step $t$')
ax.set_ylabel('Frobenius norm of $S_t$')
ax.set_title('Hidden state norm: DeltaNet vs. linear attention')
ax.legend()
plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig('deltanet_demo.png', dpi=100, bbox_inches='tight')
plt.show()

Test-Time Training and Test-Time Regression¶

The Hidden State as a Learned Model¶

Test-time training (TTT) Yu et al., 2024 takes the associative-memory perspective to its logical conclusion: the hidden state WtW_tWt​ is the weights of a small model (e.g., a linear map or a two-layer MLP) that is updated online by gradient descent during the forward pass.

For a linear model with weights Wt∈Rd×dW_t \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times d}Wt​∈Rd×d and a self-supervised reconstruction loss:

ℓt(W)=12∥Wkt−vt∥2,\ell_t(W) = \tfrac{1}{2}\|W k_t - v_t\|^2,ℓt​(W)=21​∥Wkt​−vt​∥2,
(18)

one gradient step with learning rate η\etaη gives:

Wt=Wt−1−η(Wt−1kt−vt)kt⊤.W_t = W_{t-1} - \eta (W_{t-1} k_t - v_t) k_t^\top.Wt​=Wt−1​−η(Wt−1​kt​−vt​)kt⊤​.
(19)

This is exactly the delta rule with βt=η\beta_t = \etaβt​=η. TTT with a linear model and DeltaNet with a fixed gate β\betaβ are the same algorithm, viewed through different lenses: DeltaNet is motivated by online learning of an associative memory, while TTT is motivated by gradient-based adaptation of a hidden model.

The output in TTT is produced by applying the current model to the query:

yt=Wtqt.y_t = W_t q_t.yt​=Wt​qt​.
(20)

For richer hidden models (two-layer MLP with parameters WtW_tWt​), TTT uses mini-batches of context tokens and takes multiple gradient steps, creating a meta-learned inner loop. This trades off compute for expressivity of the hidden state.

Test-Time Regression: A Probabilistic View¶

The connection to regression is made explicit by interpreting the context tokens (k1,v1),…,(kt,vt)(k_1, v_1), \ldots, (k_t, v_t)(k1​,v1​),…,(kt​,vt​) as a training set and qtq_tqt​ as a test point. The TTT linear model solves the online ridge regression problem:

Wt=arg⁡min⁡W∑s≤t∥Wks−vs∥2+λ∥W∥F2.W_t = \arg\min_W \sum_{s \leq t} \|W k_s - v_s\|^2 + \lambda \|W\|_F^2.Wt​=argWmin​s≤t∑​∥Wks​−vs​∥2+λ∥W∥F2​.
(21)

The closed-form solution is:

Wt=(∑s≤tvsks⊤)(∑s≤tksks⊤+λI)−1,W_t = \left(\sum_{s \leq t} v_s k_s^\top\right) \left(\sum_{s \leq t} k_s k_s^\top + \lambda I\right)^{-1},Wt​=(s≤t∑​vs​ks⊤​)(s≤t∑​ks​ks⊤​+λI)−1,
(22)

and the prediction yt=Wtqty_t = W_t q_tyt​=Wt​qt​ is kernel ridge regression evaluated at qtq_tqt​ with a linear kernel. From this perspective:

  • Linear attention (without denominator) computes StqtS_t q_tSt​qt​ where St=∑s≤tvsks⊤S_t = \sum_{s \leq t} v_s k_s^\topSt​=∑s≤t​vs​ks⊤​ — this is the numerator of the ridge regression estimator, ignoring the Gram matrix (∑sksks⊤+λI)−1(\sum_s k_s k_s^\top + \lambda I)^{-1}(∑s​ks​ks⊤​+λI)−1.

  • Standard attention can be interpreted as kernel regression with the softmax kernel.

  • DeltaNet / TTT tracks an online approximation to the full ridge regression solution, forgetting old training points in proportion to their alignment with the current key.

This regression perspective unifies the family of linear-complexity sequence models as variants of in-context regression with different choices of kernel, loss, and optimization algorithm.

Summary¶

The table below organises the models in this chapter by their hidden-state update rule. All achieve O(N)O(N)O(N) inference complexity; they differ in what information they can selectively retain.

ModelHidden state updateKey property
Linear attention Katharopoulos et al., 2020St=St−1+ϕ(kt)vt⊤S_t = S_{t-1} + \phi(k_t) v_t^\topSt​=St−1​+ϕ(kt​)vt⊤​No forgetting; A=IA = IA=I
S4 / S5 Gu et al., 2022 Smith et al., 2023ht=Aˉ ht−1+Bˉ xth_t = \bar{A}\, h_{t-1} + \bar{B}\, x_tht​=Aˉht−1​+Bˉxt​Fixed structured Aˉ\bar{A}Aˉ; HiPPO init
Mamba Gu & Dao, 2023ht=Aˉtht−1+Bˉtxth_t = \bar{A}_t h_{t-1} + \bar{B}_t x_tht​=Aˉt​ht−1​+Bˉt​xt​Input-dependent Aˉt,Bˉt\bar{A}_t, \bar{B}_tAˉt​,Bˉt​
DeltaNet Yang et al., 2024St=(I−βtktkt⊤)St−1+βtvtkt⊤S_t = (I - \beta_t k_t k_t^\top) S_{t-1} + \beta_t v_t k_t^\topSt​=(I−βt​kt​kt⊤​)St−1​+βt​vt​kt⊤​Online gradient descent; selective overwrite
TTT (linear) Yu et al., 2024Wt=Wt−1−η(Wt−1kt−vt)kt⊤W_t = W_{t-1} - \eta (W_{t-1}k_t - v_t)k_t^\topWt​=Wt−1​−η(Wt−1​kt​−vt​)kt⊤​Gradient descent on reconstruction loss

Two broad axes organise the design space:

  1. Selectivity: can the model gate what it remembers based on content? (A=IA = IA=I → no; Mamba, DeltaNet → yes).

  2. Expressivity of the hidden state: scalar recurrence (S4) → matrix recurrence (linear attention, DeltaNet) → MLP weights (TTT).

The dominant computational pattern — a linear recurrence that admits parallel training via associative scans or convolutions — appears across all these architectures, connecting classical signal processing, online learning, and modern sequence modeling.

References¶
  1. Katharopoulos, A., Vyas, A., Pappas, N., & Fleuret, F. (2020). Transformers are RNNs: Fast Autoregressive Transformers with Linear Attention. International Conference on Machine Learning, 5156–5165.
  2. Gu, A., Goel, K., & Ré, C. (2022). Efficiently Modeling Long Sequences with Structured State Spaces. International Conference on Learning Representations.
  3. Gu, A., & Dao, T. (2023). Mamba: Linear-Time Sequence Modeling with Selective State Spaces. arXiv Preprint arXiv:2312.00752.
  4. Yang, S., Wang, B., Shen, Y., Peng, H., & Kim, Y. (2024). Parallelizing Linear Transformers with the Delta Rule over Sequence Length. Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems, 37.
  5. Yu, Y., Guo, S., Kautz, J., Alvarez, J. M., Anandkumar, A., Xu, K., & Molchanov, P. (2024). Learning to (Learn at Test Time): RNNs with Expressive Hidden States. Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems, 37.
  6. Gu, A., Dao, T., Ermon, S., Rudra, A., & Ré, C. (2020). HiPPO: Recurrent Memory with Optimal Polynomial Projections. Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems, 33, 1474–1487.
  7. Smith, J. T. H., Warrington, A., & Linderman, S. W. (2023). Simplified State Space Layers for Sequence Modeling. International Conference on Learning Representations.
